steps.title: Actief vs Passief
steps.desc: Until now, you've always said WHO does something: 'Ik schrijf de brief' (I write the letter). But what if you want to focus on WHAT happens, not who does it? 'De brief wordt geschreven' — the letter is being written. This is the passive voice, and it's everywhere in Dutch news, rules, and formal writing.
goals: See passive in real Dutch examples
Prepare for the grammar in L2–L5

steps.title: 🔑 Active vs Passive — The Big Idea
steps.text:
Active: The DOER is the subject.
Ik schrijf de brief. = I write the letter.
De kok maakt het eten. = The cook makes the food.

Passive: The THING/ACTION is the subject.
De brief wordt geschreven. = The letter is being written.
Het eten wordt gemaakt. = The food is being made.

Why use passive?
1. You don't know who did it.
2. It doesn't matter who did it.
3. You want to sound formal or objective.
4. Rules and laws: 'Er wordt hier niet gerookt.'

Dutch uses passive MORE than English,
especially in news, regulations, and formal writing.

steps.note:
Het-word. Also: article/item in general.
'Een krantenartikel' = a newspaper article.

steps.exampleEn: The report will be presented tomorrow.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Een rapport schrijven' = to write a report.
First passive in action: 'wordt gepresenteerd'.

steps.example: De wet werd vorig jaar aangenomen.
steps.exampleEn: The law was adopted last year.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Wetten' = laws (plural).
'De wetgeving' = the legislation.
Passive is very common with laws: 'werd aangenomen'.

Het-word. 'Onderzoeken' = to research/investigate.
'Uit onderzoek blijkt dat...' = Research shows that...
Very common in news: passive + onderzoek.

You learned this in Unit 11!
Repeated because it's a passive-magnet word.
'Het besluit werd genomen door de minister.'

De-word. 'Regels' = rules.
'De regelgeving' = regulations.
Passive + modal: 'moeten worden gevolgd'.

steps.q: In 'De brief wordt geschreven', the focus is on:
steps.hint: Passive = focus on what happens, not who does it
The report is being presented tomorrow

steps.q: Passive voice is especially common in:

steps.hint: The law was adopted last year

steps.title: Worden + Gedaan
steps.desc: The present passive in Dutch uses 'worden' + past participle. 'De brief wordt geschreven' = The letter is being written. This describes a process that is happening or happens regularly.

steps.title: Worden — Present Tense
steps.label: worden (to become / passive auxiliary)
steps.note:
'Worden' has TWO jobs in Dutch:
1. To become: 'Ik word ziek' = I'm getting sick.
2. Passive auxiliary: 'De brief wordt geschreven' = The letter is being written.
Same conjugation for both uses.

steps.title: 📐 Active → Passive
steps.text:
Active: De student schrijft de brief.
           (subject)  (verb)   (object)

Passive: De brief wordt geschreven (door de student).
           (subject) (worden+PP)    (agent optional)

Steps:
1. Object → becomes the subject.
2. Verb → wordt/worden + past participle.
3. Subject → optional: 'door + ...' at end.

More examples:
De kok maakt het eten. → Het eten wordt gemaakt.
Zij leest het boek. → Het boek wordt gelezen.
Wij bespreken het plan. → Het plan wordt besproken.

steps.example: De brief wordt geschreven door de student.
steps.exampleEn: The letter is written by the student.
steps.note:
'Door' = by (in passive sentences).
Optional — only add when you WANT to say who.
'Het eten wordt gemaakt door de kok.'
Often omitted: 'Het eten wordt gemaakt.'

steps.q: 'De brief wordt geschreven.' — 'wordt' is:

steps.hint: The food IS BEING made (present passive)

steps.q: Active: 'De kok maakt het eten.' → Passive:

steps.hint: The book is read BY many people.

steps.q: When is 'door + agent' used?
opts: Only when you want to specify WHO
steps.ans: Only when you want to specify WHO

steps.s: Het plan wordt ___ door het team.
steps.hint: The plan is being discussed (PP of bespreken)

steps.title: Werd Geschreven
steps.desc: The past passive uses 'werd/werden' + past participle. 'De brief werd geschreven' = The letter was (being) written. Same pattern, just past tense of 'worden'.

steps.title: Worden — Past Tense (Imperfectum)
steps.label: werd/werden
steps.note:
Worden is a strong verb:
worden → werd/werden (past) → geworden (PP).

Present passive: De brief WORDT geschreven.
Past passive: De brief WERD geschreven.

Just swap word/wordt/worden → werd/werden!

steps.title: 📐 Present vs Past Passive
steps.text:
Present: De brief wordt geschreven.
= The letter is being written. (now/generally)

Past: De brief werd geschreven.
= The letter was (being) written. (then)

Present: Het plan wordt besproken.
Past: Het plan werd besproken.

Present: De regels worden gevolgd.
Past: De regels werden gevolgd.

The past participle stays the same!
Only worden changes: wordt → werd, worden → werden.

steps.q: 'De wet werd aangenomen.' — this happened:

steps.hint: The letter WAS written yesterday (past passive)

steps.q: Plural past passive: 'De regels _____ gevolgd.'

steps.s: Het besluit {1} genomen door de minister. De regels {2} niet gevolgd.
steps.hint: The decision WAS made. The rules WERE not followed.
The research WAS conducted last year

steps.q: Active: 'De minister nam het besluit.' → Past passive:
opts: Het besluit werd genomen door de minister.
steps.ans: Het besluit werd genomen door de minister.

steps.title: Is Geschreven
steps.desc: Dutch has TWO types of passive. You've learned the process passive (worden). Now meet the state/result passive: 'zijn' + past participle. 'De brief is geschreven' = The letter has been written (it's done). This describes the RESULT, not the process.
goals: The letter IS BEING written vs IS written

steps.title: 📐 Worden vs Zijn — Process vs Result
steps.text:
WORDEN-passive = PROCESS (it's happening):
De brief wordt geschreven. = The letter is being written.
De deur wordt gesloten. = The door is being closed.

ZIJN-passive = RESULT (it's done):
De brief is geschreven. = The letter has been written.
De deur is gesloten. = The door is closed.

Think of it as:
Worden = BECOMING (the action is underway).
Zijn = BEING (the action is complete, we see the result).

De brief wordt geschreven. → someone is writing it NOW.
De brief is geschreven. → it's DONE. Finished. Result.

This distinction doesn't exist in English!
English uses 'is written' for BOTH.
Dutch separates them: worden vs zijn.

steps.note:
Past participle of 'sluiten' (to close).
Zijn-passive: 'is gesloten' = is closed (result).
Vs worden: 'wordt gesloten' = is being closed (process).

steps.exampleEn: The new shop has been opened.
steps.note:
Past participle of 'openen' (to open).
'Is geopend' = has been opened / is open.
Note: openen → ge-open-d (regular).

Past participle of 'verbieden' (to forbid).
Zijn-passive: 'is verboden' = is forbidden.
Very common on signs: 'Verboden toegang!'

steps.q: 'De winkel is gesloten.' — this means:
opts: Someone is closing the shop now

steps.s: De brief ___ geschreven. Het is klaar! (result)

steps.q: 'De deur wordt gesloten' vs 'De deur is gesloten'. Difference?
opts: worden = process happening, zijn = result done
steps.ans: worden = process happening, zijn = result done

steps.s: De winkel {1} om negen uur geopend. Nu is hij {2}.
steps.hint: The shop IS OPENED at nine. Now it IS closed.

steps.title: Er Wordt Gefietst
steps.desc: This is the most Dutch thing about the passive voice. Dutch can make passive sentences with NO subject at all. 'Er wordt gefietst' = There is cycling / Cycling is happening. English simply can't do this. It's used for rules, general activities, and impersonal statements.
goals: No subject! Er fills the gap
Very Dutch — English can't do this

steps.title: 🚲 The Impersonal Passive
steps.text:
Normal passive has a subject:
De brief wordt geschreven. (de brief = subject)

Impersonal passive has NO real subject:
Er wordt gefietst. = There is cycling. / People cycle.
Er wordt hier gewerkt. = Work is being done here.
Er wordt niet gerookt. = No smoking. (lit: It is not smoked.)

'Er' is just a placeholder — it fills the first position.
There is no 'thing' being cycled or worked.
The verb is intransitive (no object).

This is VERY Dutch. English can't say:
'It is cycled.' ✗ (meaningless in English)
'Er wordt gefietst.' ✓ (perfectly normal in Dutch)

Used for:
1. Rules/signs: 'Er wordt hier niet gerookt.'
2. General activities: 'Er werd veel gedanst.'
3. Weather/atmosphere: 'Er wordt gewerkt.'

steps.note:
roken = to smoke. Past participle: gerookt.
Impersonal passive: er + wordt + gerookt.
'Niet' makes it a rule: no smoking.

steps.en: work is being done / people work
steps.note:
werken = to work. Impersonal passive.
No specific person — just 'work happens here'.
Very common in workplace contexts.

steps.example: Er werd veel gedanst op het feest.
steps.exampleEn: There was a lot of dancing at the party.
steps.note:
Past impersonal passive: er + werd + PP.
Present: er wordt gedanst.
Past: er werd gedanst.

steps.q: 'Er wordt hier niet gerookt.' — who is the subject?
steps.hint: Er is just a placeholder — impersonal!

steps.q: Why can English NOT say 'It is cycled' the way Dutch says 'Er wordt gefietst'?
opts: English passive needs a real subject/object
It's a grammar mistake in Dutch too
steps.ans: English passive needs a real subject/object
steps.hint: English: 'The letter is written' (subject exists). Dutch: 'Er wordt gefietst' (no subject!)

steps.s: Er werd veel ___ op het feest.
steps.hint: There was a lot of DANCING at the party

steps.title: De Krant Lezen
steps.desc: Time to see the passive voice in action. Dutch news is FULL of passive constructions. You'll read a short news text and practice recognizing passives in context.

steps.example: De minister werd gevraagd om te reageren.
steps.exampleEn: The minister was asked to respond.
steps.note:
De-word. 'De minister-president' = the prime minister.
'De ministerraad' = the cabinet/council of ministers.

De-word. 'Maatregelen nemen' = to take measures.
Very common in news: policies, regulations.
Often in passive: 'werden aangekondigd'.

Separable: 'kondigt aan' (active present).
'Aangekondigd' = past participle.
Passive: 'werd aangekondigd' = was announced.

steps.title: 📰 Lees: Het Nieuws
steps.text:
Lees het bericht en beantwoord de vragen.

Gisteren werd een nieuw plan aangekondigd door
de minister. Het plan is bedoeld om de luchtkwaliteit
te verbeteren. Nieuwe regels worden ingevoerd
voor bedrijven. Er werd veel over gesproken in
het parlement.

De maatregelen worden volgend jaar uitgevoerd.
Het besluit is genomen na lang overleg.
Niet iedereen is het ermee eens. De oppositie
vindt dat er meer onderzoek nodig is.

deepDive.title: Vertaling
deepDive.text:
Yesterday a new plan was announced by the minister.
The plan is intended to improve air quality.
New rules are being introduced for companies.
It was widely discussed in parliament.

The measures will be implemented next year.
The decision was made after lengthy consultation.
Not everyone agrees. The opposition thinks
more research is needed.

steps.q: In the text, 'werd aangekondigd' is:

steps.s: Het besluit ___ genomen na lang overleg.
steps.hint: The decision HAS BEEN made (result = zijn)

steps.q: 'Er werd veel over gesproken' — what type of passive?
steps.hint: Er + werd + PP, no real subject

steps.title: Door Wie? Waardoor?
steps.desc: Who did it? In passive sentences, 'door' (by) introduces the agent. Plus: passive with modals — 'Het moet gedaan worden' (It must be done). The final piece of the passive puzzle.
goals: Word order in sub-clauses with passive

steps.title: 📐 Passive + Modals
steps.text:
The passive can combine with modals (moeten, kunnen, etc.):

Het moet gedaan worden. = It must be done.
Dat kan niet veranderd worden. = That can't be changed.
Het zou besproken moeten worden. = It should be discussed.

Pattern: modal + past participle + worden

In a sub-clause (verb to end!):
...dat het gedaan moet worden.
...dat het besproken zou moeten worden.

The verbs stack at the end!
This is the multi-verb cluster from Unit 15 in action.

steps.example: Door wie werd het besluit genomen?
steps.exampleEn: By whom was the decision made?
steps.note:
Question: 'Door wie?' = By whom?
'Door wie werd het gedaan?'
Very common in news and formal contexts.

steps.exampleEn: What caused the problem? / By what was the problem caused?
steps.note:
waar + door = by what/which.
Used for THINGS (not people).
People: 'Door wie?' Things: 'Waardoor?'

steps.q: 'Het moet gedaan worden.' — word order is:

steps.hint: BY whom was the decision made?

steps.q: In '...dat het gedaan moet worden', why is 'worden' last?
opts: Sub-clause: all verbs go to the end
steps.ans: Sub-clause: all verbs go to the end

steps.hint: It SHOULD be discussed. (zou moeten worden)

steps.s: Het probleem ___ veroorzaakt door de storm.
steps.hint: The problem WAS caused by the storm

steps.title: Unit 16 Review
steps.desc: The full passive voice toolkit. Process passive (worden), state passive (zijn), impersonal passive (er wordt), past passive (werd/werden), passive + modals, and the door-agent. Let's test it all.

steps.q: 'De brief wordt geschreven' — which passive type?

steps.s: De brief ___ geschreven. Het is klaar.

steps.q: Active: 'De minister nam het besluit.' → Passive:
opts: Het besluit werd genomen door de minister.
steps.ans: Het besluit werd genomen door de minister.
steps.hint: Object → subject, werd + PP, door + agent
It had to be discussed. (had moeten worden)

steps.q: 'Door wie werd het gedaan?' asks:

steps.hint: The law WAS adopted last year

steps.q: Worden-passive vs zijn-passive:

steps.s: De regels {1} gevolgd. Het besluit {2} genomen.
steps.hint: The rules ARE followed. The decision HAS BEEN made.
